cases.description: Single filer: fully taxable pension, part-time W-2 wages, bank interest, and Social Security benefits — SSA taxable portion computed via IRS Worksheet 7-1
cases.source: IRS Pub 17 TY2025, Chapter 7: Social Security and Equivalent Railroad Retirement Benefits, Example 1, p. 63–64; 2025 Tax Table p. 111–122
cases.notes: IRS Pub 17 Example 1 (p. 63–64) explicitly provides: pension $18,600, wages $9,400, interest $990, SSA net $5,980. Worksheet 7-1 computation shows taxable SSA = $2,990 (line 19 of worksheet). AGI = $18,600 + $9,400 + $990 + $2,990 = $31,980. Standard deduction single TY2025 = $15,750 (Table 10-1, p. 95). Taxable income = $31,980 − $15,750 = $16,230. Tax from 2025 Rate Schedule X: $1,192.50 + 12% × ($16,230 − $11,925) = $1,192.50 + $516.60 = $1,709.10 → table rounds to $1,709. No withholding stated in example → amount_owed = total_tax.

cases.description: Married filing jointly: retired taxpayer with fully taxable pension and Social Security benefits — no SSA taxable because provisional income below MFJ threshold
cases.source: IRS Pub 17 TY2025, Chapter 7: Social Security and Equivalent Railroad Retirement Benefits, Example 2, p. 64; 2025 Tax Table p. 111–122
cases.notes: IRS Pub 17 Example 2 (p. 64): Casey has fully taxable pension $15,500, savings interest $250. Pat made deductible IRA contribution $1,000. Casey's SSA net $5,600. Provisional income = $14,750 + $2,800 (50% SSA) = $17,550 — below $32,000 MFJ threshold, so taxable SSA = $0. AGI = $15,500 + $250 − $1,000 IRA = $14,750. MFJ standard deduction = $31,500 → taxable income = $0. No tax. Line 6a = $5,600, line 6b = $0.

cases.description: Married filing jointly: retired railroad worker (RRB Tier 1 SSEB portion) and government-worker spouse with pension and savings bond interest — no SSEB taxable because provisional income below MFJ threshold
cases.source: IRS Pub 17 TY2025, Chapter 7: Social Security and Equivalent Railroad Retirement Benefits, Example 3, p. 64–65; 2025 Tax Table p. 111–122
cases.notes: IRS Pub 17 Example 3 (p. 64–65): Jamie received RRB-1099 with $10,000 SSEB. Jessie has fully taxable pension $38,000. Interest $2,500. Pub 17 example states none of their SSEB is taxable. AGI = $38,000 + $2,500 = $40,500 (SSEB excluded). MFJ standard deduction = $31,500. Taxable income = $40,500 − $31,500 = $9,000. Tax from 2025 Tax Table (MFJ, $9,000): $900 (10% × $9,000, entirely in 10% bracket). No withholding stated → amount_owed = total_tax.

cases.description: Married filing jointly: tax table worked example — IRS explicitly states taxable income $25,300 yields tax of $2,562
cases.source: IRS Pub 17 TY2025, 2025 Tax Table sample example, p. 111 (Tax Table chapter)
cases.notes: IRS Pub 17 p. 111 Tax Table explicitly states: 'A married couple... taxable income on Form 1040, line 15, is $25,300. Next, they find the $25,300–$25,350 taxable income line... the tax is $2,562.' This is IRS-provided ground truth for line 16 tax. MFJ standard deduction = $31,500, so AGI = $25,300 + $31,500 = $56,800. Wages set to $56,800 to produce this AGI exactly. Withholding $4,000 assumed for refund illustration. Refund = $4,000 − $2,562 = $1,438.

cases.description: Married filing jointly: Owen and Sophia joint return — IRS explicitly provides joint taxable income $48,500 and joint tax $5,346
cases.source: IRS Pub 17 TY2025, Chapter 4: Tax Withholding and Estimated Tax, estimated tax example, p. 41
cases.notes: IRS Pub 17 p. 41 explicitly states Owen and Sophia's joint return shows 'taxable income of $48,500 and tax of $5,346' for 2025. This is IRS-provided ground truth for line 15 and line 16/24. MFJ standard deduction $31,500 → AGI = $48,500 + $31,500 = $80,000. Wages set to $80,000. Withholding set equal to tax for break-even illustration. Tax from IRS Rate Schedule Y-1: $2,385 + 12% × ($48,500 − $23,850) = $2,385 + $2,958 = $5,343 → IRS Tax Table rounds to $5,346.

cases.description: Single filer over 65 — age-enhanced standard deduction; pension income with small Social Security partially taxable
cases.source: IRS Pub 17 TY2025, Table 10-2 standard deduction age 65+ single = $17,750 (p. 95); SSA taxable portion rules Chapter 7 p. 63; base amount $25,000 single
cases.notes: Single, age 65+. Standard deduction from Pub 17 Table 10-2 (p. 95): 1 box checked (born before Jan 2, 1961) → $17,750. Provisional income = $20,000 + $6,000 (50% of $12,000 SSA) = $26,000. Single base amount = $25,000. $26,000 > $25,000 → Worksheet 7-1: taxable SSA = 50% × ($26,000 − $25,000) = $500. AGI = $20,000 + $500 = $20,500. Taxable income = $20,500 − $17,750 = $2,750. Tax = 10% × $2,750 = $275 (from Tax Table, single, $2,750 range). Withholding $1,500. Refund = $1,500 − $275 = $1,225.

cases.description: Married filing jointly both over 65 — standard deduction with two age boxes checked; pension and interest income only
cases.source: IRS Pub 17 TY2025, Table 10-2 standard deduction MFJ with 2 boxes checked = $33,100 (p. 95); 2025 Tax Table p. 111
cases.notes: MFJ both age 65+. Standard deduction from Pub 17 Table 10-2 (p. 95): 2 boxes checked, MFJ → $31,500 base + $1,600 × 2 = $33,100. AGI = $42,000 + $1,500 = $43,500. Taxable income = $43,500 − $33,100 = $10,400. Tax = 10% × $10,400 = $1,040 (all taxable income in 10% bracket since $10,400 < $23,850 MFJ 10% ceiling). Withholding $3,000. Refund = $3,000 − $1,040 = $1,960.

cases.description: Married filing jointly: installment sale gain at 0% LTCG rate — gain from sale of investment property held over 1 year, recognized pro-rata in year of receipt
cases.source: IRS Pub 17 TY2025, Chapter 8: Other Income (p. 67–68, installment sales); Form 6252; 2025 LTCG rates (0% for MFJ taxable income ≤ $96,700); 2025 Tax Table
cases.notes: Installment sale on property held >1 year → LTCG $8,000. MFJ 2025: 0% LTCG rate applies when total taxable income ≤ $96,700. Taxable income $31,500 < $96,700, so LTCG taxed at 0%. AGI = $55,000 + $8,000 = $63,000. Standard deduction MFJ = $31,500. Taxable income = $31,500. Ordinary income portion = $31,500 − $8,000 LTCG = $23,500. MFJ 10% bracket ceiling $23,850. Tax on $23,500 ordinary = 10% × $23,500 = $2,350. LTCG tax = $0. Total tax = $2,350. Withholding $5,000. Refund = $2,650. Pub 17 p. 67–68 discusses installment sale rules; gain character from original asset.

cases.description: Single filer: cancellation of debt income — creditor forgives $6,000 student loan outside education exclusion, amount is taxable income
cases.source: IRS Pub 17 TY2025, Chapter 8: Other Income, Canceled Debts section p. 68–70; Form 1099-C; taxable COD income under IRC §61(a)(12); student loan cancellation NOT exempt if not for service/qualifying program per IRC §108(f)
cases.notes: Pub 17 Chapter 8, Canceled Debts (p. 68–70): canceled debt is generally includible in gross income (Form 1099-C box 2). The $6,000 is added on Schedule 1 line 8c. AGI = $38,000 + $6,000 = $44,000. Standard deduction single = $15,750. Taxable income = $28,250. Tax from 2025 Rate Schedule X: 10% × $11,925 = $1,192.50; 12% × ($28,250 − $11,925) = 12% × $16,325 = $1,959. Total = $3,151.50. Withholding $3,200. Refund = $48.50.

cases.description: Single filer: alimony paid deduction pre-2019 divorce decree reduces AGI — deduction on Schedule 1 line 19a
cases.source: IRS Pub 17 TY2025, alimony paid adjustment Schedule 1 line 19a; pre-2019 instruments; IRC §215; 2025 Tax Rate Schedule X (p. 125); standard deduction $15,750 (p. 95)
cases.notes: Alimony paid $18,000 deduction (pre-2019 divorce, Schedule 1 line 19a). AGI = $75,000 − $18,000 = $57,000. Single standard deduction (Pub 17 Table 10-1, p. 95) = $15,750. Taxable income = $41,250. Tax using 2025 Rate Schedule X (Pub 17 p. 125): 10% × $11,925 = $1,192.50; 12% × ($41,250 − $11,925) = 12% × $29,325 = $3,519. Total = $4,711.50. Withholding = $8,000. Refund = $3,288.50.

cases.description: Single filer: hobby loss limitation — income from hobby fully includible, but related expenses not deductible (post-TCJA no misc itemized deductions)
cases.source: IRS Pub 17 TY2025, Chapter 12: Other Itemized Deductions, Hobby Expenses section p. 102; hobby loss rules IRC §183; post-TCJA: hobby expenses not deductible as misc itemized deduction; all hobby income taxable
cases.notes: Pub 17 p. 102: hobby expenses are miscellaneous itemized deductions no longer allowed post-TCJA. All hobby income must be reported in full (Schedule 1 line 8j). Hobby income $3,500 added to income; zero offset for expenses. AGI = $42,000 + $3,500 = $45,500. Standard deduction = $15,750. Taxable income = $29,750. Tax from Rate Schedule X: $1,192.50 + 12% × ($29,750 − $11,925) = $1,192.50 + 12% × $17,825 = $1,192.50 + $2,139 = $3,331.50. Withholding $4,000. Refund = $668.50.

cases.description: Head of household: standard deduction $23,625 with one qualifying child and Child Tax Credit
cases.source: IRS Pub 17 TY2025, Table 10-1 HOH standard deduction $23,625 (p. 95); Chapter 14 CTC $2,000 per child (p. 108); 2025 Tax Rate Schedule Z HOH (p. 125)
cases.notes: HOH standard deduction = $23,625 (Pub 17 Table 10-1, p. 95). Taxable income = $45,000 − $23,625 = $21,375. 2025 HOH Tax Rate Schedule Z (Pub 17 p. 125): 10% × $17,000 = $1,700; 12% × ($21,375 − $17,000) = 12% × $4,375 = $525. Income tax = $2,225. Child Tax Credit: $2,000 per qualifying child (Chapter 14, p. 108) — nonrefundable. $2,225 − $2,000 = $225. No ACTC (CTC fully absorbed into regular tax). Total tax = $225. Withholding $3,500. Refund = $3,275.

cases.description: Married filing separately: Owen's separate return — IRS explicitly provides taxable income $40,100 and tax $4,577
cases.source: IRS Pub 17 TY2025, Table 10-1: MFS standard deduction = $15,750 (p. 95); MFS filing restrictions; Owen filing separately: taxable income $40,100, tax confirmed per p. 41
cases.notes: Pub 17 p. 41 explicitly states Owen files separately with taxable income $40,100 and tax of $4,577. MFS standard deduction = $15,750 (Table 10-1, p. 95). AGI = $40,100 + $15,750 = $55,850. Tax from 2025 Rate Schedule Y-2 (MFS, Pub 17 p. 125): 10% × $11,925 = $1,192.50; 12% × ($40,100 − $11,925) = 12% × $28,175 = $3,381; total = $4,573.50 → IRS Tax Table rounds to $4,577. IRS explicitly provides $4,577 for Owen's separate return — ground truth from Pub 17 p. 41.
